summary: Google Tasks API does NOT support push notifications or webhooks of any kind — polling is the only option, confirmed by both official API schema and Google's own Home Assistant integration maintainer.

The correct polling strategy is timestamp-based: store a last-sync datetime, then call tasks.list with updatedMin=<RFC3339 timestamp> plus showDeleted=true and showHidden=true to capture all change types.
There is NO syncToken mechanism in the Tasks API (unlike Calendar API) — the response schema only contains nextPageToken for pagination, not nextSyncToken.

The collection-level etag field in the tasks.list response CAN be used for cache-validation (If-None-Match header), but only to detect whether ANY change occurred in the list — not to identify which tasks changed.

Quota is 50,000 queries/day (courtesy limit). No per-second hard limit is documented, but Google enforces undisclosed short-burst rate limits — a 60-second polling interval is safe; 30-second is likely fine; sub-10-second risks throttling.
The Google Workspace Events API (which provides push subscriptions for Chat, Meet, Drive) does NOT support Google Tasks as a subscribable resource as of April 2026.

findings.key_finding: The tasks.list endpoint accepts updatedMin (RFC 3339 timestamp) as the only change-detection parameter. There is no syncToken parameter or nextSyncToken in the response. The response schema is: {kind, etag, nextPageToken, items[]}. This confirms timestamp-polling as the definitive strategy.

The complete API schema for the Google Tasks v1 API has been inspected via the official googleapis Go client JSON descriptor. No syncToken field exists anywhere in the Tasks API — neither in request parameters nor response schemas. This is a confirmed absence, not an undocumented feature.

The Google Workspace Events API supports push notification subscriptions via Cloud Pub/Sub for Google Chat, Meet, and Drive only. Google Tasks is explicitly absent from the supported resource types. There is no indication this will change in the near term.

Home Assistant's Google Tasks integration maintainer (allenporter) explicitly confirmed: 'Google tasks API only supports polling and does not have any other APIs to receive notifications on changes more rapidly.' The integration uses a 30-minute default polling interval. This is a real-world authoritative confirmation.

The Google Tasks API has a courtesy limit of 50,000 queries per day per project. No per-user per-second limit is explicitly published. The default is described as 6,000,000 requests per minute per project (a project-level ceiling, not a meaningful Tasks-specific constraint). Quota increases can be requested via Cloud Console.

Each Task resource has an 'updated' field (RFC 3339, output-only) representing last modification time, a per-task 'etag' string, a 'deleted' boolean flag (for soft-deleted tasks), and a 'hidden' boolean. The 'status' field is either 'needsAction' or 'completed'. These fields are what a poller should track and compare.

Only two release notes exist: June 2018 (GA launch) and July 2024 (support for tasks from Google Docs/Chat). No synchronization, webhook, or incremental-sync features have ever been added to the Tasks API. The API is functionally unchanged for change-detection purposes since 2018.

Third-party integration guide confirms updatedMin timestamp polling as the canonical pattern. Recommends storing last sync time, calling tasks.list with updatedMin=lastSyncTime.toISOString(), then advancing the timestamp. No mention of any alternative mechanisms.

The Google Calendar API (a related Google Workspace API) supports syncToken-based incremental sync with nextSyncToken in the response. This pattern is absent from Tasks API, confirming that the Tasks API is a less capable API that was never extended with this mechanism.

gaps: Per-user per-second rate limit for Tasks API is not published. Google Cloud Console shows quota details only after enabling the API in a project — the precise burst limit is empirically determined.

Whether the collection-level etag in tasks.list response changes on any task modification (making it usable as a cheap 'anything changed?' check before fetching full data) could not be definitively confirmed from documentation alone — requires empirical testing.

It is unclear whether updatedMin filters tasks by their server-side 'updated' timestamp (write time) or by a logical modification time. If Gemini on-device creates a task offline and syncs later, the 'updated' timestamp used by the API may be the sync time or the creation time — needs testing.
The maximum safe polling frequency without hitting rate limits is not documented. The 30-minute interval used by Home Assistant is conservative; actual limits for burst polling are unknown.
Google has not published a roadmap for Tasks API webhook support, so it is unknown if/when push notifications will be added.
delta_queries: Test empirically: does tasks.list etag change when a single task is modified? (GET /tasks/v1/lists/{id}/tasks, record etag, modify a task, GET again — compare etags)
Test If-None-Match header support: send GET tasks.list with If-None-Match: <previous_etag> — does the API return 304 Not Modified when nothing has changed?
Search 'Google Tasks API future roadmap push notifications 2025 2026' to check for announced plans
Check Google Issue Tracker for open feature requests: https://issuetracker.google.com/issues?q=tasks+api+webhook
Verify whether Gemini app tasks on Android/iOS appear in the default task list or a dedicated list — this affects which tasklist IDs to poll

recommended_polling_strategy.interval: 60 seconds recommended (conservative); 30 seconds acceptable for personal use
recommended_polling_strategy.on_new_tasks: Filter items where updated > lastSyncTime; write to Obsidian vault
recommended_polling_strategy.on_deleted_tasks: items[].deleted == true — mark vault task as deleted/archived
recommended_polling_strategy.advance_cursor: After processing, set lastSyncTime = now() (or max(items[].updated))
recommended_polling_strategy.quota_math: At 60s interval: 1440 calls/day per tasklist — well within 50,000/day limit; at 30s: 2880/day per list
